content.subtitle: One situation, different choices
content.description: The same future event can often be expressed with more than one structure. The form you choose depends on meaning, speaker intention, and style.
examples: The seminar will start at 10 tomorrow.
The seminar is starting at 10 tomorrow.
The seminar is going to start at 10 tomorrow.

content.subtitle: What affects your choice?
items: Function: prediction, offer, decision, request, promise, refusal, arrangement, timetable, etc.
How fixed the event feels: planned, scheduled, uncertain, or based on present evidence.
Formality: **will** is often more formal than **be going to** in many contexts.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Predictions
Future facts
Decisions made at the moment of speaking
Offers
Promises
Requests
Refusals (won't)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: I think travel costs will rise again next year.
The results will be published on Monday.
That phone is ringing — I'll get it.
I'll carry those boxes for you.
I won't share your draft with anyone.
Will you check this final paragraph for me?
No, I won't agree to that deadline.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: With **I/we** in offers and suggestions, **shall** is common: **Shall I open the window? / Shall we leave now?**
For requests, **would/could** are usually more polite than **will/can**.
For refusals, **couldn't/can't** may be softer than **won't** in some contexts.

content.subtitle: British usage note
items: In British English, **shall** is sometimes used with **I/we** instead of **will** in formal style.
Example: **I shall contact you once the review is complete.**

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Predictions based on present evidence
Plans or intentions already in mind

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Look at those black clouds — it's going to storm.
I'm going to apply for a master's programme next autumn.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Present continuous: personal arrangements
Present simple: timetables, programmes, fixed schedules
Present continuous is less common for very distant or uncertain plans

content.subtitle: Examples
items: I'm meeting the admissions tutor on Thursday afternoon.
We're flying to Dublin next weekend.
The train leaves at 6:45 am.
The conference opens on 14 June.
I'm working abroad one day. (less natural for distant or uncertain plans)

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: For distant ambitions, **be going to** is often more natural than present continuous.
Compare: **I'm going to work abroad one day** (more natural) vs **I'm working abroad one day** (less natural).

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Future perfect simple: completed action before a future time
Future perfect simple: completed states/situations by a deadline
Future perfect continuous: activity continuing up to a future time (focus on duration)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: By 8 p.m., they will have finished the final edit.
By next July, she will have worked here for ten years.
By next July, she will have been working here for ten years.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Action in progress at a specific future time
Expected event in the normal course of things
Repeated or characteristic behaviour around a future period

content.subtitle: Examples
items: This time tomorrow I'll be presenting our findings.
Professor Khan will be joining us after lunch.
In a few years, more teams will be collaborating entirely online.

content.subtitle: Core rule
content.description: After time linkers such as **when, before, after, once, as soon as, until**, we normally use present forms in the time clause (present simple, present continuous, present perfect simple/continuous as needed), not **will** or **be going to**.
examples: I'll call you when I arrive.
We'll decide while we're reviewing the alternatives.
She'll apply once she's completed the portfolio.
They won't confirm anything until they've reviewed all the evidence.

content.subtitle: Be careful
items: Use **will** in the main clause if needed, but normally not inside the time clause itself.
Correct: **I'll text you when I get home.**
Incorrect pattern: **I'll text you when I'll get home.**

sections.title: Other Ways to Express the Future

useItems: very near future, often dramatic context
examples: She is on the verge of announcing the result.
The panel is due to publish its report tomorrow.
All candidates are to remain in the hall until 12:00.
They might delay the launch if funding is reduced.

content.subtitle: Backshifting future forms
content.description: When reporting a future idea from a past viewpoint, forms usually shift back: **will → would**, **is going to → was going to**, present forms for scheduled future events often shift to past forms.
examples: Then: I think the committee will approve it. → Later: I thought the committee would approve it.
Then: We're going to present on Friday. → Later: We were going to present on Friday.
Then: The bus leaves at six. → Later: She said the bus left at six.
